nativeRange.habitat: Tropical and subtropical freshwater rivers, streams, and reservoirs as adults; larvae are obligately brackish and develop in estuarine and lower-river habitats before migrating upstream as post-larvae.

decorPreferences: abundant cover and refuges (rocks, driftwood, clay pots, PVC pipes)
soft to medium gravel substrate suitable for digging
robust filtration with shrimp-safe intake guards
dolomite or crushed coral to maintain alkalinity and calcium

diet.notes: Generally regarded as an opportunistic omnivore with strong predatory tendencies, the Giant Freshwater Prawn has been documented accepting shrimp pellets, sinking carnivore wafers, frozen and freeze-dried foods (bloodworms, brine shrimp, tubifex, krill, squid, mussel, fish pieces), blanched vegetables, and supplementary plant matter such as water sprite or seaweed sheets. Calcium- and lecithin-rich foods are commonly recommended to support successful molting. Flake-only diets are widely considered inadequate.

goodWith: fast-swimming mid- and top-water fish too large to be caught (when kept in very large tanks)
tankmates introduced before the prawn reaches adult size
avoidWith: other adult male Macrobrachium rosenbergii (territorial and combative)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — treated as prey
snails (mystery, nerite, ramshorn) — predated and shells crushed
slow or bottom-dwelling fish (corydoras, plecos, loaches)
small or peaceful nano fish (tetras, rasboras, livebearer fry)
any tankmate small enough to be ambushed
crayfish and other large crustaceans (escalating territorial fights)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by KKPCW, 2048x1536. Sharp full-body 3/4 view of a live specimen in an aquascaped tank with the diagnostic elongated blue chelipeds (Blue Claw male) clearly displayed against rock and gravel.
Photo by Jarek Tuszynski, 3872x2592. Crisp high-res full lateral profile showing the whole animal, banded abdomen and blue-tinged legs; clean reference angle.
Photo by Jarek Tuszynski, 3872x2121. Dramatic head-on view emphasizing the massively elongated blue second chelipeds spread wide; sharp, conveys the species' claw span.

summary: The Giant Freshwater Prawn is a very large Indo-Pacific species that can reach 30 cm or more in body length. Primarily an aquaculture species, it is widely considered a specialist-only animal in the home hobby due to its size, predatory nature, and territorial behavior.
careNotes:
The Giant Freshwater Prawn is a specialist-only species, not a typical home-aquarium prawn. Adult males have been documented to exceed 30 cm in body length, with claws that can reach roughly twice the body length, and are widely regarded as aggressive toward conspecifics. They will hunt almost any tankmate small enough to catch — including dwarf shrimp, snails, and most peaceful community fish. A species-only setup housing a single adult, or at most a carefully sexed pair in a very large tank with abundant cover, is generally the only realistic configuration.

Like all decapod crustaceans, the Giant Freshwater Prawn is generally considered highly copper-sensitive. Copper-based medications, snail treatments, and copper-bearing tap water should all be avoided. The species climbs readily and is a strong escape risk; a tightly fitted lid with any cable-pass gaps sealed is commonly recommended. It is also reported to be very sensitive to chlorine and chloramine, and to require well-aerated water, as extended periods of low oxygen have been documented to suppress appetite.

Calcium supplementation through crushed coral, dolomite, or calcium-rich foods — along with dietary lecithin — is commonly cited as important for successful molting. A condition known as 'molt death syndrome' has been linked in aquaculture sources to soy-lecithin deficiency. Adult males fall into three distinct morphotypes — Small Male, Orange Claw, and Blue Claw — that form a strict dominance hierarchy, with Blue Claw males dominant.

LEGAL STATUS — READ BEFORE PURCHASE. The Giant Freshwater Prawn has been introduced and become invasive in parts of Africa, the Americas, the Caribbean, China, Japan, and New Zealand following accidental aquaculture releases. It is legally restricted in some jurisdictions — prohibited in the state of Maine, USA, for example — and keepers should confirm local regulations before purchase. The species must never be released into wild waterways.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Giant Freshwater Prawn is generally considered impractical in a standard home aquarium because the larval stages are obligately brackish — meaning the newly hatched young cannot survive in fresh water. Females have been documented to extrude 10,000 to 50,000 eggs per clutch, with up to roughly five clutches per year, and carry the eggs under the abdomen for under three weeks until hatching. The newly hatched larvae (zoeae) must be transferred to brackish water of approximately 12-16 ppt salinity held at 28-31°C, where they pass through several larval stages over roughly 32-35 days before metamorphosing into post-larvae that can gradually be acclimated back to fresh water. Commercial hatcheries replicate this cycle routinely, but the infrastructure involved makes it rare in home settings. Among males, only the dominant Blue Claw morphotype consistently sires offspring in a given tank, with Orange Claw and Small Male individuals subordinate in the hierarchy.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Designated secondary; no species-specific page for Macrobrachium rosenbergii was found (HTTP 404 on expected slug and no matching result in site search). Substituted with Aquatic Arts, Miami Aquaculture, TFH Magazine, FAO and Wikipedia.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Substitute primary: retailer care profile (listed as M. spinipes, the Australian cherabin form historically synonymized with M. rosenbergii). Source for tank-size floor (40+ US gal per specimen), temperature 20-28°C, pH 7.0-8.0, gH 3-15, kH 1-7, advanced care level, brackish larval requirement, copper/handling notes.

additional.name: Miami Aquaculture (Pet Prawns)
additional.notes: Substitute secondary: pet-prawn care guide. Cross-confirmed temperature 26-30°C optimum (22-32°C survival), pH 7.2-8.4, alkalinity/calcium dependence, chlorine sensitivity, molting frequency (juveniles 2-3 days, adult females 20-40 days), territorial behaviour from ~5 cm, sensitivity of dwarf shrimp and snails as tankmates.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Hobbyist Magazine (Freshwater Prawns)
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed maximum size (near small-lobster scale, arms up to 2x body length), native Indo-Pacific/Australasian range, predator status, 'not an animal for the casual aquarist' care-level signal.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Macrobrachium rosenbergii) + FAO Freshwater Prawn Farming Manual
additional.notes: Taxonomy (family Palaemonidae, order Decapoda), synonymy and trade names (Malaysian Prawn, Scampi, Cherabin, Udang Galah, Golda Chingri), maximum sizes (males ~34 cm, females ~26 cm), native range countries, fecundity (10,000-50,000 eggs), larval brackish requirement (12-16 ppt salinity at 28-31°C, ~32-35 days). FAO manual: https://www.fao.org/4/y4100e/y4100e04.htm.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Reported molting intervals vary widely with age. Juvenile post-larvae have been documented molting roughly every 2-3 days, while adult females are reported to molt approximately every 20-40 days. Successful molting is widely considered to depend on stable, alkaline water with adequate calcium and dietary lecithin; a condition known as 'molt death syndrome' has been linked in aquaculture sources to soy-lecithin deficiency. The discarded exoskeleton is often consumed by the prawn for its calcium content and should generally be left in the tank. Regenerating lost claws or legs typically requires 1-2 molt cycles.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Effectively impractical in a single-tank home setup because larvae must be reared in brackish water of ~12-16 ppt at 28-31°C for ~32-35 days. See top-level breedingNotes for details.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Adults exceed 30 cm and are aggressive, territorial predators that will eat or injure most tankmates and other prawns. Requires a very large species-only tank with abundant cover and a secure lid. Breeding is impractical at home because larvae are obligately brackish.
